summary: SKGLElement (WPF) has a memory leak because its Dispose() does not call base.Dispose() on GLWpfControl, and the referenced OpenTK versions (4.3.0 / 4.2.3) have an upstream memory leak in GLWpfControl itself.

stepsToReproduce: Create a WPF application using SKGLElement
Dispose the SKGLElement (e.g., close the window or reload the control)
Observe memory growth using a profiler or Task Manager

repoLinks.description: Minimal WPF repro project demonstrating memory growth
Upstream OpenTK GLWpfControl memory leak issue (fixed in later versions)
PR #3311: Updates OpenTK dependency versions to fix this leak

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: Current codebase uses OpenTK 4.3.0/GLWpfControl 4.2.3 which have the upstream leak. Dispose() still doesn't call base.Dispose().

analysis.summary: Two compounding bugs cause the memory leak: (1) SKGLElement.Dispose() calls Dispose(true) but never calls base.Dispose(), so GLWpfControl's resources are never freed; (2) the referenced OpenTK GLWpfControl 4.2.3 has a separate upstream memory leak fixed in 4.3.3+. PR #3311 addresses both by updating the OpenTK package references.
analysis.rationale: Code investigation confirms Dispose() in SKGLElement does not call base.Dispose(). The csproj references OpenTK 4.3.0 and OpenTK.GLWpfControl 4.2.3 which are known to have the upstream memory leak. The reporter provided a minimal repro project. PR #3311 is open and directly addresses the root cause.

keySignals.text: SKGLElement is disposed it does not call the Dispose method of the base class, this causes memory leaks
keySignals.interpretation: Reporter correctly identified that base.Dispose() is not called — confirmed by reading SKGLElement.cs

keySignals.text: One solution is to update the OpenTK dependency
keySignals.interpretation: The upstream GLWpfControl had a separate memory leak also fixed by the newer OpenTK version

keySignals.text: You can see the memory leak is gone with #3311
keySignals.interpretation: Reporter already submitted PR #3311 that fixes both issues

codeInvestigation.finding: Dispose() calls Dispose(true) via the standard IDisposable pattern, but the Dispose(bool) method does NOT call base.Dispose(). GLWpfControl inherits from FrameworkElement which has disposal logic that never gets invoked.
For non-net4 targets, references OpenTK 4.3.0 and OpenTK.GLWpfControl 4.2.3. The upstream GLWpfControl issue #126 (memory leak) was fixed in GLWpfControl 4.3.3+ which requires OpenTK 4.9.4.

workarounds: Manually update OpenTK to 4.9.4 and OpenTK.GLWpfControl to 4.3.3 in the consuming app's project file using package override/redirect
Override/subclass SKGLElement and call base.Dispose() in your own Dispose override
nextQuestions: Does calling base.Dispose() in the older OpenTK versions cause any issues, or only the newer versions expose it properly?
Is this leak also present in the net4x target (OpenTK 3.3.1 / GLWpfControl 3.3.0)?

resolution.hypothesis: Dispose() does not propagate to the GLWpfControl base class, so OpenTK's internal OpenGL resources (textures, framebuffers, GL contexts) are never released.

proposals.title: Update OpenTK package references and call base.Dispose()
proposals.description: Bump OpenTK to 4.9.4 and OpenTK.GLWpfControl to 4.3.3 (these versions expose Dispose and fix the upstream leak). Also add base.Dispose() call in SKGLElement.Dispose(bool). PR #3311 already does this.

resolution.recommendedProposal: Update OpenTK package references and call base.Dispose()
resolution.recommendedReason: Root cause is clear — missing base.Dispose() call and outdated OpenTK with known upstream leak. PR #3311 already fixes both. Effort is minimal.

actionability.reason: Root cause clearly identified in code. PR #3311 is open with the fix. The leak is confirmed by the reporter's repro project and validated by code investigation.

actions.description: Apply bug, WPF views, Windows, OpenGL, and reliability labels
Link to PR #3311 which fixes this issue
Acknowledge the bug and reference the fix PR
actions.comment:
Thanks for reporting this and for the repro project! The root cause is confirmed: `SKGLElement.Dispose()` does not call `base.Dispose()`, so `GLWpfControl`'s internal OpenGL resources are never released. Additionally, the current referenced OpenTK version (`4.3.0` / `GLWpfControl 4.2.3`) has a separate upstream memory leak that was fixed in [GLWpfControl#126](https://github.com/opentk/GLWpfControl/issues/126).

PR #3311 addresses both issues by updating the OpenTK package references to `OpenTK 4.9.4` + `OpenTK.GLWpfControl 4.3.3`.

**Workaround until the fix is released:** You can override the OpenTK packages in your app:
```xml
<PackageReference Include="OpenTK" Version="4.9.4" NoWarn="NU1701" />
<PackageReference Include="OpenTK.GLWpfControl" Version="4.3.3" />
```
